content.text: Skills and connectors become significantly more powerful when they are designed as a unit. A skill template that references a specific connector is useful — but only if the user has that connector authorized. When you bundle skills and connectors together in a plugin, you create a cohesive workflow package: install once, authorize the connectors once, and every skill in the bundle is immediately ready to use.

This topic covers the mechanics of bundling: how to declare connectors in a plugin manifest, how skill templates reference bundled connectors, what happens at authorization time, and how to design bundles that are reusable across many teams without being too prescriptive.

attrs.alt: Bundled skills and connectors plugin diagram

content.text: Why Bundle Skills and Connectors

When skills and connectors are installed separately, users must know which connectors a skill needs and authorize them manually before running. This breaks the workflow and creates friction, especially for new team members who may not know which connectors are required for each skill.
The separate-install problem

Without bundling — what a new team member experiences:
1. Admin installs the Sales plugin (skills: /deal-memo, /call-prep)
2. New user runs /deal-memo for the first time
3. Goal fails: "Gmail connector not authorized"
4. User searches docs to find which connector is needed
5. User goes to Settings → Connectors → Gmail → Connect
6. User runs /deal-memo again
7. Goal fails again: "Drive connector not authorized"
8. User repeats the cycle for Drive

With bundling:
1. Admin installs the Sales plugin
2. Plugin installer shows: "This plugin requires Gmail, Drive, and Chrome — authorize now"
3. New user authorizes all three connectors in one step at install time
4. /deal-memo works immediately on first invocation
Bundling makes the dependency explicit at install time rather than at run time. Users authorize all required connectors once, not per-skill-invocation-per-connector.
When to bundle vs keep separate
Scenario
Bundle?
Reason
Multiple skills all need the same connector
Yes
One authorization covers all skills; cohesive package
Skills are general-purpose and used with many different connectors
No
Skills without connector dependency are maximally reusable
Internal API connector not in the base app
Yes
Must bundle to make the connector available at all
Skills occasionally use a connector but work without it
No
Conditional connector use works better without bundling
Building a marketplace plugin for wide distribution
Yes — with minimal connector scope
Broad compatibility; request only the scopes you need
Declaring Connectors in the Plugin Manifest

A plugin's connector declarations are part of the plugin manifest — the definition file that describes the plugin's components. In the Cowork plugin editor, you add connectors in the "Connectors" section by selecting from the available connector types and specifying the minimum required OAuth scope.
Connector declaration fields
Connector type: one of the supported types (Gmail, Drive, Slack, Chrome, or a custom connector)
Required scopes: the specific OAuth permissions the plugin's skills need (read-only vs read/write, specific folders vs full access)
Display name: the human-readable name shown during authorization ("Read your Gmail for account research")
Optional/required flag: whether the plugin fails gracefully without this connector or requires it to function
Minimal scope principle

Declare only the OAuth scopes your skill templates actually use. A skill that reads email threads does not need write access to Gmail. A skill that reads a specific Drive folder does not need full Drive access.

Minimal scope is not just a security best practice — it makes your plugin more likely to be installed. Users and workspace admins are reluctant to install plugins that request broad permissions. A plugin requesting read-only Gmail access gets installed far more readily than one requesting full Gmail control.
Example: Sales Intelligence plugin manifest

Plugin: Sales Intelligence
Version: 1.2.0

Connectors:
  - type: Gmail
    scope: read_threads          # read email threads — no write access
    required: false              # skills degrade gracefully without it
    display: "Read your Gmail to find account email history"

  - type: Google Drive
    scope: read_write_named_folder  # read + write, but scoped to a folder
    required: true
    display: "Write deal memos and call prep docs to your Drive"

  - type: Chrome
    scope: page_read             # read rendered page content only
    required: false
    display: "Load competitor websites for competitive intelligence"

Skills:
  - name: deal-memo
    requires_connectors: [gmail, drive]
  - name: call-prep
    requires_connectors: [gmail, drive, chrome]
  - name: competitive-brief
    requires_connectors: [chrome, gmail, drive]

Drive is declared required because all three skills write output to Drive. Gmail and Chrome are optional — skills will note when these are unavailable and produce partial output rather than failing entirely.
Writing Skill Templates for Bundled Connectors

Skill templates in a bundled plugin reference connectors by type, not by credential — the connector configuration is provided by the bundle. A template says "read the Gmail connector" and the engine uses the OAuth-authorized Gmail account configured at plugin install time.
Referencing connectors in templates

Skill: /sales-deal-memo
Template:

Prepare a deal memo for {{account_name}} ({{company_domain}}).

1. [Gmail connector] Search for all email threads involving @{{company_domain}} 
   in the past 90 days. Extract: key stakeholders, open commitments, 
   stated objections, any pricing discussions.

2. [Drive connector] Check the "Accounts / {{account_name}}" folder in Drive
   for any existing account notes or previous memos. Read them for context.

3. Write a deal memo with sections:
   - Account Overview
   - Stakeholders (names, roles, email addresses from Gmail)
   - History & Context (from Drive notes + email)
   - Objections & Open Items
   - Recommended Next Step

Save the memo to the "Deal Memos" folder in Drive as "{{account_name}} — Deal Memo — [date]".

If Gmail is not authorized, skip section 1 and note "Email history unavailable" in the memo.
The
[Gmail connector]
and
[Drive connector]
labels in the template text are instructions to the engine, not technical syntax. The engine matches these to the plugin's declared connectors and uses the authorized accounts.
Handling optional connectors in templates

When a connector is declared optional in the plugin manifest, the skill template must include fallback instructions for when it is not authorized. This is different from a required connector, where the engine can stop and prompt for authorization.
Optional connector pattern: describe what to do with it, then describe what to do without it
Required connector pattern: assume the connector is available; let the engine handle missing auth as an error
Testing: test each skill with its optional connectors both authorized and not authorized — the fallback path should produce useful partial output, not an empty document
Cross-connector skill patterns
The most valuable bundled skills combine connectors in ways that would require significant manual coordination to replicate. The canonical patterns:
Pattern
Connectors
What it accomplishes
Read → synthesise → write
Any read source + Drive
Gather from one connector, synthesise, store in Drive for future reference
Monitor → alert
Chrome or Gmail + Slack
Watch for a condition, post notification when triggered
Read → enrich → write
Gmail/Drive (read) + Chrome (enrich) + Drive (write)
Gather internal context, enrich with web research, produce combined output
Aggregate → report
Multiple read connectors + Drive/Slack
Pull from several sources, compile unified report, distribute
Authorization Flow at Plugin Install Time

When a user installs a plugin that declares connectors, Cowork presents an authorization step immediately after installation. This is the moment where the bundle's value is realized — the user authorizes all required connectors in one flow rather than encountering authorization errors one by one during skill invocations.
Authorization step flow
User installs the plugin from marketplace or workspace library
Cowork shows: "This plugin requires authorization for N connector(s)" with each connector listed
For required connectors: user must authorize before the plugin activates
For optional connectors: user can skip — the skill picker shows a warning indicator for skills that need the unauthorized connector
User completes OAuth for each connector (redirected to the service's consent screen)
Plugin activates — all skills appear in the picker, all required connectors are active
Re-authorization and token refresh
Connector OAuth tokens expire (typically 60–90 days for Google services, 30 days for some Slack configurations)
When a token expires, skills that depend on that connector show a warning in the picker: "Connector needs re-authorization"
Re-authorize from Settings → Plugins → [plugin name] → [connector] → Re-authorize
Re-authorization does not require reinstalling the plugin — it only refreshes the token
Workspace admins can see which connectors across the workspace have expired tokens in the admin connector health panel
Building a Bundle for Distribution

A bundled plugin intended for distribution — whether to a workspace or the marketplace — requires more careful design than a personal plugin. The bundle must work reliably for users who have different account structures, folder names, and permission levels.
Design for unknown folder structures

Do not hardcode specific folder paths in skill templates. Users may have different Drive structures, and a template that assumes a "Deal Memos" folder at the root will fail for users who organize Drive differently.

Bad: Save to the "Deal Memos" folder in Drive.
(Fails if the user does not have a folder with that exact name)

Better: Save to a new Google Doc named "{{account_name}} — Deal Memo — [date]"
in the root of Drive, or in the "{{output_folder}}" folder if specified.

Best: Save to a new Google Doc named "{{account_name}} — Deal Memo — [date]".
If the plugin resource "output-config.json" specifies a target folder, use that path.
Otherwise save to the Drive root.
Version your bundle
Use semantic versioning: major.minor.patch (1.0.0, 1.1.0, 2.0.0)
A major version bump (1.x → 2.x) signals breaking changes to skill template behavior
Include a changelog in the plugin description — users who depend on a skill's output format need to know when it changes
For workspace plugins, notify the team before deploying a major version update — skill templates that have changed will produce different output, which may break downstream workflows
Testing a bundle before distribution
Install the plugin in a personal or sandbox workspace first
Authorize all declared connectors — verify the authorization flow completes without errors
Run each skill with typical inputs — verify output format and destination
Run each skill with the optional connectors disabled — verify the fallback path produces valid partial output
Run each skill with edge-case inputs (long names, special characters, empty source folders)
Check that required connector authorization failure produces a useful error message rather than a silent empty output
Only submit to the workspace or marketplace after all six steps pass
Example: Engineering Incident Bundle
A complete example showing how to design and document a bundled plugin for an engineering team's incident management workflow.
Bundle definition

Plugin: Engineering Incident Response
Version: 1.0.0

Connectors:
  - type: PagerDuty (custom, team-built)
    scope: read_incidents        # read active incidents + escalation history
    required: true
    display: "Read PagerDuty incidents and escalation data"

  - type: Slack
    scope: post_messages         # post to channels
    required: true
    display: "Post incident briefs and postmortem summaries to Slack"

  - type: Google Drive
    scope: read_write            # read runbook templates + write incident docs
    required: true
    display: "Read runbook templates and write incident documents to Drive"

Skills:
  - name: eng-incident-brief
    requires_connectors: [pagerduty, slack, drive]

  - name: eng-postmortem
    requires_connectors: [pagerduty, drive]

  - name: eng-runbook-draft
    requires_connectors: [drive]

Instructions:
  Every incident brief must include a severity classification (P0–P3)
  using the criteria defined in the severity-matrix resource.
  
  Every postmortem must include a root cause section with at least 3 levels
  of "why" analysis.

Resources:
  severity-matrix.md     — P0–P3 definitions, SLAs, and escalation contacts
  runbook-template.md    — standard runbook structure for new systems
What users experience at install time
Admin installs Engineering Incident Response plugin at workspace scope
Cowork shows authorization step: "This plugin requires 3 connectors: PagerDuty, Slack, Google Drive"
Admin authorizes all three (or delegates authorization to individual team members)
Plugin is active — /eng-incident-brief, /eng-postmortem, /eng-runbook-draft appear in the picker
Severity matrix and runbook template resources are embedded and immediately accessible
Plugin instructions (severity classification rule, postmortem depth rule) are active for all workspace goals
Skill: /eng-incident-brief

Template:

Draft an incident brief for PagerDuty incident {{incident_id}}.

1. Read the PagerDuty incident record for {{incident_id}}.
   Extract: title, severity assigned by PagerDuty, affected systems,
   current status, timeline of events, responders on-call.

2. Classify severity using the criteria in the severity-matrix resource
   (P0: full outage; P1: significant degradation; P2: partial impact; P3: minor).
   If PagerDuty severity differs from the criteria-based classification, note both.

3. Write a brief with:
   - Incident ID and title
   - Severity (criteria-based, with PagerDuty classification noted if different)
   - Impact: affected systems and estimated user count
   - Current status
   - Timeline (bullet points, most recent first)
   - Next step (who is doing what right now)

Save the brief to a new Google Doc in the "Incidents / [date]" folder in Drive.
Post the impact + current status section (2–3 sentences) to #eng-incidents in Slack.
Summary

Bundling skills and connectors in a plugin creates a cohesive, installable workflow unit that eliminates the per-skill-invocation authorization friction. The bundle declares all required connectors upfront, the user authorizes them once at install time, and every skill in the bundle is immediately ready to use.
Declare only the OAuth scopes each connector actually needs — minimal scope improves installability and trust
Mark connectors as required or optional; skill templates must include fallback instructions for optional connectors
Cross-connector patterns (read → enrich → write; monitor → alert) are where bundled plugins provide the most value
Design templates for unknown user folder structures — avoid hardcoded paths
Version bundles semantically and document breaking changes in changelogs
Test with optional connectors both authorized and unauthorized before distributing
